tweets.text:
US small businesses worried about the economy:

The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index fell -0.6 points in May, to 95.3, the lowest since October 2024.

Year-to-date, this figure has declined -4.2 points, already surpassing the 2025 full-year decrease of -3.3 points.

The % of small business owners expecting better business conditions has declined -21 percentage points this year, to 3%, the lowest since October 2024.

Furthermore, just 7% of small firms believe it is a good time to expand, also the lowest since October 2024.

All while taxes, inflation, and labor costs remain the most cited problems among small business owners.

Outlook among US small businesses is bleak.

tweets.text:
BREAKING: Foreign holdings of US Treasuries rose +$3.9 billion in April, to $9.35 trillion, the 2nd-highest on record.

Japan, the largest foreign holder, raised its holdings +$18.3 billion, to $1.21 trillion, the highest since February.

The UK, the 2nd-largest holder, added +$10.6 billion, to a record $937.5 billion.

China, the 3rd-largest holder, cut its holdings by -$1.2 billion, to $651.1 billion, the lowest since September 2008.

This also marks China's 3rd consecutive monthly decline, totaling -$43.3 billion.

Meanwhile, Canada, the 7th-largest foreign holder, cut its holdings by -$42.3 billion, to $397.1 billion, the lowest since January.

The US Treasury market is growing but remains fragmented.

tweets.text:
Technology firms are reshaping the global investment landscape:

The IT sector now accounts for ~38% of the MSCI USA Index, which tracks large and mid-cap stocks of the US equity market, covering ~85% of the US market cap.

This percentage has surged +15 points over the last 4 years.

The IT sector also accounts for ~44% of the MSCI EM Index, which covers large and mid-cap stocks across 24 emerging market countries, representing ~85% of each country's market cap.

Since 2022, tech's contribution in this metric has more than doubled.

Over the same period, the IT sector's proportion of US investment-grade bond issuance has more than tripled, to a record ~20%.

Tech is all that matters right now.

tweets.text:
🚨 Private credit is not collapsing but is clearly going through its first real liquidity test.

📈 We are witnessing a sharp acceleration in redemption requests across several large private-credit funds from just over $7 billion in Q1 2026 to almost $12 billion in Q2. The asset class has grown massively on very attractive promises such as high yields, low volatility, and more stability than public markets, but investors are now rediscovering that it remains an illiquid asset class.

⚠️ However, it is important to remind that high redemption requests do not mean the entire private-credit market is about to blow up. Many funds remain solid and strong managers can navigate this phase without major issues, but dispersion will increase. Funds with real credit discipline, limited leverage, strong covenants and a stable investor base should be more resilient while more aggressive vehicles, or those too dependent on inflows, will be much more vulnerable.

Private credit is not disappearing but the market is simply reminding everyone that when you accept less liquidity to earn more yield, you must be ready to bear that illiquidity when everyone wants to exit at the same time.

*WSJ Link: https://t.co/TabNeBdwd4

thread_tweets.text:
Here, I get paid zero per post (because I choose to opt out of pay-per-engagements on this platform).

Likely if I got paid per engagement, my memes and short controversial statements would be my main income and my longer, thoughtful threads would underperform.
thread_tweets.full_text:
Here, I get paid zero per post (because I choose to opt out of pay-per-engagements on this platform).

Likely if I got paid per engagement, my memes and short controversial statements would be my main income and my longer, thoughtful threads would underperform.

thread_tweets.text:
In that Nostr environment, you know exactly what you got paid for every post.

Some posts there earn me zero or a few cents. Like for a meme or short comment.

Other rare ones earn me thousands of dollar-equivalents. I wrote something deep, heartfelt, actionable, rare, powerful.
thread_tweets.full_text:
In that Nostr environment, you know exactly what you got paid for every post.

Some posts there earn me zero or a few cents. Like for a meme or short comment.

Other rare ones earn me thousands of dollar-equivalents. I wrote something deep, heartfelt, actionable, rare, powerful.

thread_tweets.text:
Introducing tips would be a potential approach.

Folks on the small decentralized social media protocol Nostr have had that for a while with zap tips.

As I've seen there, fun-but-shallow posts get likes, while deep posts that punch above their weight get real-money zap tips.
thread_tweets.full_text:
Introducing tips would be a potential approach.

Folks on the small decentralized social media protocol Nostr have had that for a while with zap tips.

As I've seen there, fun-but-shallow posts get likes, while deep posts that punch above their weight get real-money zap tips.

thread_tweets.text:
Options:

-You can incentivize virality for posting things people view as bad but are popular/controversial.

-You can incentivize enjoyment for good but shallow things (cats).

-You can incentivize deep/thoughtful posts where people learned and want to tip.
thread_tweets.full_text:
Options:

-You can incentivize virality for posting things people view as bad but are popular/controversial.

-You can incentivize enjoyment for good but shallow things (cats).

-You can incentivize deep/thoughtful posts where people learned and want to tip.

thread_tweets.text:
The value of tips is that you can't game them, especially if you publicly itemize where tips came from as Nostr clients generally do.

And bots paying most users tips doesn't make a ton of sense. It's expensive.

It's often real personal value transferred, in other words.
thread_tweets.full_text:
The value of tips is that you can't game them, especially if you publicly itemize where tips came from as Nostr clients generally do.

And bots paying most users tips doesn't make a ton of sense. It's expensive.

It's often real personal value transferred, in other words.

thread_tweets.text:
For example, @Devon_Eriksen_  writes posts on X, often quite long-form. They influence people, sometimes significantly.

Agree or disagree with him, those who agree are sometimes like, "I'd pay ten dollars for this".

But they wouldn't do that for a meme with 10x the engagement.
thread_tweets.full_text:
For example, @Devon_Eriksen_  writes posts on X, often quite long-form. They influence people, sometimes significantly.

Agree or disagree with him, those who agree are sometimes like, "I'd pay ten dollars for this".

But they wouldn't do that for a meme with 10x the engagement.

thread_tweets.text:
In general, I think quality-oriented metrics are better incentives, and one of the best mechanisms for that is to let the users decide what to transfer value to (tips, zaps, etc).

People "like" (costless) and  actually "pay for" (costfull) different things.
thread_tweets.full_text:
In general, I think quality-oriented metrics are better incentives, and one of the best mechanisms for that is to let the users decide what to transfer value to (tips, zaps, etc).

People "like" (costless) and  actually "pay for" (costfull) different things.

thread_tweets.text:
And unlike decentralized networks, centralized networks run into all sorts of regulation issues with tips. Money transmitter licenses, KYC, etc.

In contrast, a decentralized social network can enable BTC/LN payments without a central platform to call upon. It's a swarm.
thread_tweets.full_text:
And unlike decentralized networks, centralized networks run into all sorts of regulation issues with tips. Money transmitter licenses, KYC, etc.

In contrast, a decentralized social network can enable BTC/LN payments without a central platform to call upon. It's a swarm.

thread_tweets.text:
There's a trade-off.

As a platform, do you maximize raw engagements with a given thing, e.g. "look at these two people fighting <insert racial claim>"

Or do you optimize quality like, "I want to tip this person's post" or "this person has 10k posts with no community notes."
thread_tweets.full_text:
There's a trade-off.

As a platform, do you maximize raw engagements with a given thing, e.g. "look at these two people fighting <insert racial claim>"

Or do you optimize quality like, "I want to tip this person's post" or "this person has 10k posts with no community notes."

thread_tweets.text:
Unlike decentralized places such as Nostr, centralized places generally don't have an incentive in that quality/tip direction. That's why it's not here.

So, they want to 1) keep you on the platform as much as possible, and 2) rile you up with sensationalism while you're here.
thread_tweets.full_text:
Unlike decentralized places such as Nostr, centralized places generally don't have an incentive in that quality/tip direction. That's why it's not here.

So, they want to 1) keep you on the platform as much as possible, and 2) rile you up with sensationalism while you're here.

thread_tweets.text:
That's why I don't turn on monetization on this platform, despite my scale. In fact, it's partially *because of* my scale.

For better or worse, my posts are my own reasonings.

Raw engagement numbers, as big as they are at times,  mean little to me.

Quality metrics do. https://t.co/ITbOG0y0qe

tweets.full_text:
My main concern with that level of detail is it gives more transparency to those who'd try to play the system.

I suspect that's why @nikitabier doesn't do it.

And that feeds back into why I dislike pay-per-engagement. If you can't make it transparent, there's likely a reason.

It's funny.

I don't think pay-per-engagement is good for incentives (it encourages sensationalism), which is why I haven't turned it on after all this time despite now having ~1 million followers.

But I agree with Devon that if it exists, it should be transparent+itemized. 🧵

Here, I get paid zero per post (because I choose to opt out of pay-per-engagements on this platform).

Likely if I got paid per engagement, my memes and short controversial statements would be my main income and my longer, thoughtful threads would underperform.

In that Nostr environment, you know exactly what you got paid for every post.

Some posts there earn me zero or a few cents. Like for a meme or short comment.

Other rare ones earn me thousands of dollar-equivalents. I wrote something deep, heartfelt, actionable, rare, powerful.

Introducing tips would be a potential approach.

Folks on the small decentralized social media protocol Nostr have had that for a while with zap tips.

As I've seen there, fun-but-shallow posts get likes, while deep posts that punch above their weight get real-money zap tips.

Options:

-You can incentivize virality for posting things people view as bad but are popular/controversial.

-You can incentivize enjoyment for good but shallow things (cats).

-You can incentivize deep/thoughtful posts where people learned and want to tip.

The value of tips is that you can't game them, especially if you publicly itemize where tips came from as Nostr clients generally do.

And bots paying most users tips doesn't make a ton of sense. It's expensive.

It's often real personal value transferred, in other words.

For example, @Devon_Eriksen_  writes posts on X, often quite long-form. They influence people, sometimes significantly.

Agree or disagree with him, those who agree are sometimes like, "I'd pay ten dollars for this".

But they wouldn't do that for a meme with 10x the engagement.

In general, I think quality-oriented metrics are better incentives, and one of the best mechanisms for that is to let the users decide what to transfer value to (tips, zaps, etc).

People "like" (costless) and  actually "pay for" (costfull) different things.

And unlike decentralized networks, centralized networks run into all sorts of regulation issues with tips. Money transmitter licenses, KYC, etc.

In contrast, a decentralized social network can enable BTC/LN payments without a central platform to call upon. It's a swarm.

There's a trade-off.

As a platform, do you maximize raw engagements with a given thing, e.g. "look at these two people fighting <insert racial claim>"

Or do you optimize quality like, "I want to tip this person's post" or "this person has 10k posts with no community notes."

Unlike decentralized places such as Nostr, centralized places generally don't have an incentive in that quality/tip direction. That's why it's not here.

So, they want to 1) keep you on the platform as much as possible, and 2) rile you up with sensationalism while you're here.

That's why I don't turn on monetization on this platform, despite my scale. In fact, it's partially *because of* my scale.

For better or worse, my posts are my own reasonings.

Raw engagement numbers, as big as they are at times,  mean little to me.

Quality metrics do. https://t.co/ITbOG0y0qe

@brian_trollz Agreed.

Tips are the raw, and probably best super-like. A transfer of value, chosen by the giver.

Centralized platforms (where tips are hard with regulation) can still learn things from this scarcity, and aim toward similar directions with their bigger scale. Super-likes.

@brian_trollz Yes, and some of those super-likes can filter toward payments.

Or toward rare skins. Which, aside from vanity, serve as useful anti-impersonator visuals.

There are many variables to optimize toward more quality, anti-fakery, etc than just current likes and comments.
